# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains empirical evidence about how developers write persistent rule files for AI coding assistants in open-source repositories, including dataset construction, coding method, taxonomy structure, prevalence of context categories, duplication/reuse patterns, and variation by language, domain, and repository age.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: method
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The study qualitatively analyzed 401 open-source repositories containing cursor rules.

## Source Excerpt

“This paper presents a large-scale empirical study to characterize this emerging form of developer-provided context. Through a qualitative analysis of 401 open-source repositories containing cursor rules...”

## Note

The source frames the study as large-scale and empirical, but this evidence unit only captures the stated study scope.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: result
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The authors organized developer-provided context into five high-level themes: Conventions, Guidelines, Project Information, LLM Directives, and Examples.

## Source Excerpt

“...we developed a comprehensive taxonomy of project context that developers consider essential, organized into five high-level themes: Conventions, Guidelines, Project Information, LLM Directives, and Examples.”

## Note

This unit captures the reported taxonomy outcome, not whether the taxonomy is exhaustive beyond this dataset.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: method
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.1 Data Collection and Preprocessing
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Initial repository fetching found 487 repositories with at least one .mdc file before cleaning.

## Source Excerpt

“We fetched repositories containing at least one cursor rule file (i.e., with the .mdc extension), excluding any forked or archived repositories, which resulted in 487 repositories.”

## Note

This is the pre-cleaning repository count.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: method
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.1 Data Cleaning
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The final analysis set was reduced to 401 repositories after multiple manual cleaning steps.

## Source Excerpt

“This left us with 418 repositories for further analysis... we removed 12 such repositories after manual review. Additionally... we removed five such repositories after manual review, leaving 401 repositories for further analysis.”

## Note

The final dataset depends on manual exclusions for deleted files, non-rule .mdc uses, non-English files, duplicates, and template/example repositories.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.2 Dataset
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The 401 repositories contained 1,876 .mdc files, averaging 4.68 .mdc files per repository.

## Source Excerpt

“Among all repositories, we identified a total of 1,876 .mdc files. On average, each repository contained 4.68 .mdc files (SD = 6.89), with the maximum number of .mdc files in a single repository being 56.”

## Note

This is a dataset composition metric for collected rule files.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: metric
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.2 Dataset
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
93.27% of repositories placed cursor rule files in the recommended root .cursor directory.

## Source Excerpt

“We found that 374 repositories (93.27%) followed this recommendation.”

## Note

The recommendation referenced is placing cursor rule files in a dedicated .cursor directory at the repository root.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: metric
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.2 Dataset
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The average cursor rule file length was 462.67 lines, with the longest file at 11,076 lines.

## Source Excerpt

“The average length of all cursor rule files was 462.67 lines (SD = 1,197.00), with the longest file containing 11,076 lines.”

## Note

This evidence describes observed file length, not effective context size after exclusions in coding.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: method
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.3 Qualitative Analysis
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The initial open coding sample used 30 repositories, selected as 3 repositories from each of 10 popular topics.

## Source Excerpt

“We then selected the top 10 most popular topics... To prevent class imbalance, we randomly selected 3 repositories from each topic, resulting in a total of 30 repositories.”

## Note

This unit captures the sampling design for initial open coding.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: metric
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.3 Qualitative Analysis
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Independent coding of the initial 30 repositories by two researchers reached Cohen’s Kappa of 0.71.

## Source Excerpt

“Finally, independent coding of the initial 30 repositories by two researchers yielded substantial agreement... (Cohen’s Kappa = 0.71).”

## Note

This metric reports inter-rater agreement for the human coding stage.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: method
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.3 Qualitative Analysis
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The full-dataset coding used gemini-2.5-flash-preview-09-2025 with temperature 0.0, repeated labeling three times, and majority vote per line.

## Source Excerpt

“We used gemini-2.5-flash-preview-09-2025 with temperature 0.0 and dynamic thinking enabled. To enhance reliability, we repeated the labeling process three times and selected the majority vote as the final label for each line. If all three labels were different, we marked it as ‘No Code’ to indicate uncertainty.”

## Note

This evidence matters to interpretation because part of the coding pipeline depended on LLM-assisted labeling and an uncertainty fallback rule.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.3 and 3.4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
On the initial 30 repositories, LLM-assisted coding reached Cohen’s Kappa values of 0.64 and 0.65 against the two human raters.

## Source Excerpt

“To validate the LLM-assisted coding, we ran this process on the initial 30 repositories and found substantial agreement between the LLM and both human raters (Cohen’s Kappa = 0.64 and 0.65)...”

## Note

These agreement values show partial alignment with human coding, not equivalence; the source later notes possible LLM bias or misinterpretation.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: metric
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.4 Threats to Validity
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
2.1% of all lines had three different LLM labels and were marked “No Code,” and the authors say this should be treated as a lower bound.

## Source Excerpt

“If all three labels were different, we marked it as ‘No Code’ to indicate uncertainty. There are 2.1% of all lines fall into this disagreement. However, considering the size of our dataset, this number should be treated as a lower bound.”

## Note

This is an uncertainty indicator in the coding process and may understate total disagreement.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: metric
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Table 1 and Sec. 4.4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Project context appeared in 85% of repositories, Guideline in 89%, Convention in 84%, and LLM Directives in 50%.

## Source Excerpt

From Table 1: “Convention... 0.84”; “Guideline... 0.89”; “Project... 0.85”; “LLM Directives... 0.50.” Section 4.4 also states: “Only about 50% of repositories (compared to 85% for Project, 84% for Convention, and 89% for Guideline) contain LLM-specific instructions...”

## Note

These are repository-level prevalence rates for top-level categories.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: metric
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 5.3 Context Co-occurrence
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
37.16% of repositories provided four content categories, and 24.69% provided all five categories including examples.

## Source Excerpt

“In general, developers were motivated to provide comprehensive context for LLMs, with 149 repositories (37.16%) providing four actual content context categories in their cursor rules and 99 repositories (24.69%) providing all five context categories (including examples).”

## Note

This unit reports category co-occurrence at repository level.
```

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: metric
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 5.4 Proportion of Duplicated Lines
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The dataset contained 19,917 duplicated lines, equal to 28.70% of all lines across repositories.

## Source Excerpt

“Overall, we identified a total of 19917 (28.70%) duplicated lines across all repositories with an average of 49.67 duplicate lines (SD=164.20) per repository.”

## Note

The source ties duplicated lines to reuse from documentation, similar repositories, templates, and some apparent LLM-generated text, so this evidence carries a reuse/copying caveat for interpreting rule originality.
---

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: method
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.3 Qualitative Analysis
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The full-dataset coding used gemini-2.5-flash-preview-09-2025 with temperature 0.0, repeated labeling three times, and majority vote per line.

## Source Excerpt

“We used gemini-2.5-flash-preview-09-2025 with temperature 0.0 and dynamic thinking enabled. To enhance reliability, we repeated the labeling process three times and selected the majority vote as the final label for each line. If all three labels were different, we marked it as ‘No Code’ to indicate uncertainty.”

## Note

This evidence matters to interpretation because part of the coding pipeline depended on LLM-assisted labeling and an uncertainty fallback rule.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-11

citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.3 and 3.4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
On the initial 30 repositories, LLM-assisted coding reached Cohen’s Kappa values of 0.64 and 0.65 against the two human raters.

## Source Excerpt

“To validate the LLM-assisted coding, we ran this process on the initial 30 repositories and found substantial agreement between the LLM and both human raters (Cohen’s Kappa = 0.64 and 0.65)...”

## Note

These agreement values show partial alignment with human coding, not equivalence; the source later notes possible LLM bias or misinterpretation.
```

2.1% of all lines had three different LLM labels and were marked “No Code,” and the authors say this should be treated as a lower bound.

```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-022
evidence_id: evidence-14

citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 5.3 Context Co-occurrence
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.18925
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
37.16% of repositories provided four content categories, and 24.69% provided all five categories including examples.

## Source Excerpt

“In general, developers were motivated to provide comprehensive context for LLMs, with 149 repositories (37.16%) providing four actual content context categories in their cursor rules and 99 repositories (24.69%) providing all five context categories (including examples).”

This unit reports category co-occurrence at repository level.
```

---

evidence_id: evidence-15

---
The dataset contained 19,917 duplicated lines, equal to 28.70% of all lines across repositories.

## Source Excerpt

“Overall, we identified a total of 19917 (28.70%) duplicated lines across all repositories with an average of 49.67 duplicate lines (SD=164.20) per repository.”

## Note

The source ties duplicated lines to reuse from documentation, similar repositories, templates, and some apparent LLM-generated text, so this evidence carries a reuse/copying caveat for interpreting rule originality.

# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains empirical evidence about how developers write persistent rule files for AI coding assistants in open-source repositories, including dataset construction, coding method, taxonomy structure, prevalence of context categories, duplication/reuse patterns, and variation by language, domain, and repository age.
